findings.title: Abstract/intro tout exact filtering; sole real-data result shows the regime-blind K=1 Kalman filter beats every GSS variant
findings.description: The framing sections sell the exact H5-IMM filter as the central deliverable (abstract: 'runs without any approximation'; intro contribution 1; conclusion: 'fast, exact optimal filter'). But the only real-data benchmark reports that a single Kalman filter ignoring regimes achieves BOTH the lowest test MSE AND the highest log-likelihood/obs, beating all regime-aware GSS filters. The exact filter's headline advantage (best test log-likelihood = -30.09) holds only *among the regime-aware filters*, a qualifier buried in the body and absent from abstract/conclusion. A reader of the abstract/conclusion alone would not learn that on the paper's sole real dataset the proposed machinery is dominated by a textbook baseline.
findings.evidence: 07_real_data.tex:131-135 'The single Kalman filter (K=1) achieves both the lowest test MSE and the highest log-likelihood per observation ... an important caveat'; vs abstract:18-21 'runs without any approximation'; 08_conclusion.tex:10 'admits a fast, exact optimal filter.'
findings.suggestion: Add the K=1-dominance caveat (or at least 'among regime-aware filters') to the conclusion's empirical summary, and soften the abstract's claim that the framework is the operative win on real data.

findings.title: Abstract sells the exact filter as removing IMM error, but simulation gains are negligible (RMSE within 0.1-0.5%) on every model
findings.description: The abstract/intro frame the Gaussian-collapse approximation as 'the only source of suboptimality' that the method eliminates. Yet the simulation study shows the exact filter and approximate IMM are 'nearly identical' on M1 (within 0.1% RMSE), 'near-identical' on M3, and differ by only 0.4595 vs 0.4616 RMSE on M2 (~0.5%). The accuracy benefit demonstrated is essentially in the noise; the real measured win is CPU (~1.4x), which the abstract never mentions. This is a contribution-vs-evidence mismatch: the headline ('removes the approximation') is technically true but the body shows the approximation was practically harmless in all tested configurations.
findings.evidence: 06_experiments.tex:162-163 'both filters achieve nearly identical RMSE (within 0.1%...)'; :197-198 '0.4595 vs 0.4616'; :205 'both filters again perform near-identically in RMSE'; vs 01_introduction.tex:24 'the only source of suboptimality'.
findings.suggestion: Temper the abstract/intro to state the exactness yields a guaranteed-correct covariance/likelihood and a CPU saving, while empirically the RMSE gap over standard IMM is small in the tested regimes; report it honestly rather than implying a large accuracy win.

findings.title: Abstract lists Σ_U among the five blocks that determine A and B; body and proof state A,B are independent of Σ_U
findings.description: The abstract says the AB constraint 'determines A and B jointly from the remaining five blocks (C, D, Σ_U, Σ_V, Δ)'. But Sec 4 explicitly states A and B are determined 'independently of Σ_U' (04_constraint.tex:79), the boxed formula A=ΔΣ_V^{-1}C, B=ΔΣ_V^{-1}D contains no Σ_U, the BIC count treats only the five blocks, and the appendix proof confirms no Σ_U dependence. The intro contribution 2 even lists only four blocks '(C, D, Δ, Σ_V)'. So three different block lists circulate: abstract = {C,D,Σ_U,Σ_V,Δ}, intro = {C,D,Δ,Σ_V}, Sec 4 body = '(C, D, Σ_U, Σ_V, Δ) independently of Σ_U'. Including Σ_U as a *determinant* of A,B in the abstract is misleading.
findings.evidence: 00_abstract.tex:32-33 'from the remaining five blocks (C, D, \Sigma_U, \Sigma_V, \Delta)'; vs 04_constraint.tex:78-79 'determined by the remaining five blocks (C, D, \Sigma_U, \Sigma_V, \Delta), independently of \Sigma_U' and eq:AB containing no Σ_U; vs 01_introduction.tex:81 'determined jointly from (C, D, \Delta, \Sigma_V)'.
findings.suggestion: Use one consistent statement everywhere: A,B are determined by (C,D,Δ,Σ_V) only; Σ_U is a free block of the model but does not enter A,B. Fix the abstract to not list Σ_U as a determinant.

findings.title: BIC model-order-selection subsection presents a method but delivers no result; only one BIC number, K=1,3,4 deferred
findings.description: Section 6.6 introduces a full BIC apparatus (parameter-count formula eq:bic_d, BIC definition) as if model-order selection were a contribution, but then states that the comparison values for K=1,3,4 are 'deferred to a companion computational study'. Only a single BIC number for the true K=2 is reported, which selects nothing. This is a half-delivered item: it occupies a numbered subsection and derives machinery whose payoff is explicitly punted. Neither the intro's four-contribution list nor the conclusion mentions BIC/model selection at all, so it is body material that is both unfinished and unadvertised.
findings.evidence: 06_experiments.tex:390-393 'A proper BIC-based model order selection requires fitting H5-GSS models with K=1,3,4 ... this is deferred to a companion computational study'; the four contributions in 01_introduction.tex:57-100 do not list model selection.
findings.suggestion: Either complete the K-sweep, or demote the BIC material to a brief remark stating the parameter count is available for future model selection, avoiding a subsection that promises selection and delivers none.

findings.title: 'generically necessary' claim survives in abstract+intro+conclusion with no supporting argument anywhere (counting argument absent from Appendix C)
findings.description: This is the same family as the acknowledged 'K·s>=q+s' issue, but note that the weaker phrasing in the abstract and conclusion ('generically necessary', dropping the inequality) is ALSO unsupported: Appendix C (app:AB_constraint) proves only sufficiency (Prop AB_satisfies_H5) and a uniqueness-of-elimination argument that assumes the constraint must hold 'across the cone of admissible Σ(r)≻0'. There is no genericity/counting argument establishing necessity for fixed structural blocks. The three framing sections assert necessity as fact while the appendix delivers only sufficiency plus a 'for all Σ' elimination (which is a different and stronger premise than 'generic'). Flagging because the abstract/conclusion phrasing is a distinct location from the acknowledged intro inequality.
findings.evidence: 00_abstract.tex:33-34 'sufficient — and generically necessary — for (H5)-compatibility'; 08_conclusion.tex:18-19 'always sufficient ... and generically necessary in all configurations'; Appendix C contains only Prop AB_satisfies_H5 (sufficiency) and the 'across the cone of Σ≻0' elimination, no genericity statement.
findings.suggestion: Restate uniformly as: the AB form is the unique solution valid for all Σ(r)≻0 (the 'free-covariance' notion the appendix actually proves), and drop 'generically necessary' unless a genuine genericity argument for fixed blocks is added.

findings.title: 'the only source of suboptimality' / 'almost all subsequent work' stated as fact without citation support
findings.description: The intro asserts the Gaussian collapse 'is the only source of suboptimality in the algorithm and the entry point for almost all subsequent work on improved JMLS filters'. The first half is defensible for the idealized IMM, but the sweeping 'almost all subsequent work' is an unsupported universal claim about a large literature, backed only by two particle-filter citations (doucet_particle_2001, andrieu_particle_2003). Particle/RBPF methods are not primarily motivated by removing the IMM collapse; many target nonlinear/non-Gaussian dynamics. This is a 'first/only/all'-type overclaim.
findings.evidence: 01_introduction.tex:24-26 'This Gaussian-collapse approximation is the only source of suboptimality in the algorithm and the entry point for almost all subsequent work on improved JMLS filters~\cite{doucet_particle_2001,andrieu_particle_2003}'.
findings.suggestion: Soften to 'a primary source of suboptimality' and 'motivates much subsequent work', or cite a survey that actually frames the literature this way.

findings.title: Financial-signal (S&P500/VIX) H5-rejection claim 'p<10^-4' is asserted with no experiment, table, or appendix backing it
findings.description: To contrast ENSO's H5-compatibility, the text claims the same F-test 'rejects (H5) at p<10^-4 for the dominant regime' on daily S&P500/VIX. No such experiment is reported anywhere in the paper (no table, figure, data description, or appendix). It is a concrete numerical claim about an unshown dataset used rhetorically to bolster the ENSO result. Either it is an unreported result (reproducibility gap) or an anecdote stated with false precision.
findings.evidence: 07_real_data.tex:98-100 'contrasts with financial signals (e.g., daily S&P500/VIX), where the same test rejects (H5) at p<10^-4 for the dominant regime.' No S&P500/VIX experiment appears elsewhere.
findings.suggestion: Either add the financial experiment (even briefly, in an appendix) or remove the specific p-value and present the contrast qualitatively without an unbacked number.

findings.title: Conclusion lists 'exact smoothing' as future work, but the EM M-step already uses smoothing posteriors (γ,ξ)
findings.description: Future-work item (i) proposes 'Extending the estimation procedure to exact smoothing, by replacing the filtering posteriors with the smoothing posteriors in the M-step.' But the semi-supervised EM in Sec 5 already runs forward-backward and uses the smoothed posteriors γ_n(k)=p(r_n|z_{1:N}) and ξ_n(j,k) in the M-step (05_estimation.tex:94-119). So the M-step is not based on filtering posteriors; the stated future direction mischaracterizes what the body already does. The intended direction is presumably RTS-style state smoothing of X, not regime smoothing — but as written it contradicts the delivered method.
findings.evidence: 08_conclusion.tex:34-35 'replacing the filtering posteriors with the smoothing posteriors in the M-step'; vs 05_estimation.tex:94-95 'smoothed posteriors \gamma_n(k):=p(r_n=k|z_{1:N})' already used in eq:M_step_Theta.
findings.suggestion: Reword to clarify the future work is exact *state* (RTS) smoothing of X within each regime, since the regime-posterior smoothing in the M-step is already implemented.

findings.title: Abstract calls IMM the 'workhorse filter' and frames whole paper around IMM, but the filter is explicitly 'not a new filter' — a re-expression of the companion paper
findings.description: The abstract and contribution 1 present the exact-IMM filter as a paper deliverable ('runs without any approximation'), but Sec 3 states plainly 'The recursion below is therefore not a new filter; it is a re-expression ... of the recursion derived in [companion]' (03_filtering.tex:19-22). The novelty is the IMM *reading* and the bias extension, not the filter. The abstract's phrasing ('we show that this approximation is unnecessary ... the standard IMM cycle ... runs without any approximation') reads as a new filtering result rather than a re-derivation. Minor, but the editorial altitude of the abstract overstates the filtering contribution relative to the body's honest disclaimer.
findings.evidence: 03_filtering.tex:19-22 'not a new filter; it is a re-expression, in the language of Kalman filtering and IMM, of the recursion derived in~\cite{derrode_fast_2026}'; vs abstract:17-21 framing the IMM cycle as a result of this paper.
findings.suggestion: Add a half-sentence to the abstract crediting the companion paper for the underlying filter and positioning this paper's filtering contribution as the IMM reformulation + affine-bias extension.

findings.title: H5 residual formula uses undefined matrix W: '||Δ^T A^T + Σ_V B^T - P M^{-1} W||_F'
findings.description: The reference-model definition gives the H5 residual as ||Δ^T A^T + Σ_V B^T - P M^{-1} W||_F < 1e-10. The compact constraint in Sec 4 (eq:H5_compact) is Δ^T A^T + Σ_V B^T = P M^{-1}(Q A^T + R B^T + Δ^T); there is no matrix 'W' in the constraint. 'W' is the process-noise vector everywhere else in the paper (eq:dynamics), so using 'W' for the inner bracket (Q A^T + R B^T + Δ^T) is an undefined/colliding symbol in a verification formula readers may want to reproduce.
findings.evidence: 06_experiments.tex:18-19 '\|\Delta^T A^T + \Sigma_V B^T - PM^{-1}W\|_F < 10^{-10}'; the inner bracket in eq:H5_compact (04_constraint.tex:44) is (Q A^T + R B^T + Δ^T), not 'W'.
findings.suggestion: Replace 'W' with the explicit bracket (QA^T+RB^T+Δ^T) or define a named symbol for it, avoiding reuse of W.

dimensions.scope_summary: Audited editorial coherence and overclaims across the abstract (00), introduction (01), conclusion (08), cross-checked against the body (02 model/H5, 03 filtering, 04 AB-constraint, 05 estimation, 06 simulation, 07 ENSO real-data) and appendices A (time reversal), B (H5 derivation), C (AB proof). Focus: claims in abstract/intro/conclusion not supported by the body, abstract-conclusion inconsistencies, contributions announced but not delivered (or body results never surfaced), novelty/optimality/exactness overclaims, and key-term consistency (H5 vs AB vs exact/approx). Excluded all previously acknowledged items (b_X/b_Y in eq:mu_jk, the 'K·s>=q+s' counting gap as stated in the intro, 'unique closed-form solution' oversell, P/R/b/W/Σ_W notation collisions, mixed index conventions, dead macros, \\yN vs \\yn, ref/label issues). Reported 10 NEW findings. Highest-impact: (1) the sole real-data experiment shows the regime-blind K=1 Kalman filter beating every proposed GSS filter, a caveat absent from abstract/conclusion; (2) simulation accuracy gains over standard IMM are negligible (<=0.5% RMSE) yet abstract frames exactness as a substantive win; (3) abstract lists Σ_U among the blocks determining A,B though body+proof state A,B are Σ_U-independent (three inconsistent block lists across sections). Also: an unfinished/unadvertised BIC model-selection subsection, an unbacked 'p<10^-4' S&P500/VIX claim, 'generically necessary' asserted in abstract/conclusion without any genericity argument in the appendix, 'only source of suboptimality / almost all subsequent work' unsupported universal, a future-work item ('exact smoothing in the M-step') that contradicts the already-implemented smoothed-posterior M-step, the abstract overstating the filtering contribution vs Sec 3's 'not a new filter' disclaimer, and an undefined matrix 'W' in the H5-residual verification formula in Sec 6.

findings.title: Three .bib entries defined but never cited (dead bibliography entries)
findings.description: Three entries exist in paper.bib but are cited nowhere in sections/, appendix/, or paper.tex. They do not appear in paper.bbl (18 bibitems vs 21 defined entries) or in the \bibcite records of paper.aux, so they silently vanish from the compiled bibliography. Either cite them where relevant or remove them. Note ghahramani_variational_2000 (Switching State-Space Models) and rabiner_tutorial_1989 (HMM tutorial) are highly relevant to the EM/Baum-Welch discussion in section 05, and horn_matrix_2013 (Matrix Analysis) is the natural reference for the Schur complement actually invoked in appendix B (see separate finding) — so this is more likely a missing-citation oversight than truly unused refs.
findings.evidence: paper.bib defines 21 keys; only 18 are cited. Uncited: ghahramani_variational_2000 (line 160), horn_matrix_2013 (line 213), rabiner_tutorial_1989 (line 138). Verified via `grep -rn` over sections/ appendix/ paper.tex: '(no usage)' for all three. paper.blg line 16: 'You've used 18 entries'. paper.bbl has 18 \bibitem.
findings.suggestion: Cite rabiner_tutorial_1989 and bilmes/baum cluster together at sections/05_estimation.tex:75; cite ghahramani_variational_2000 in the intro/EM discussion of switching SSMs; cite horn_matrix_2013 at the Schur complement in appendix/B_h5_derivation.tex:74. Otherwise delete the unused entries.

findings.title: Mangled author rendering in .bbl from thin-space (\,) inside name initials
findings.description: Four author fields use a LaTeX thin-space \, between given-name initials (e.g. 'A.\,P.'). IEEEtran.bst treats the literal characters in the name field per BibTeX name-parsing rules and mis-tokenizes 'A.\,P.' so the trailing initial is emitted as a spurious 'von'/last-name fragment. The compiled bibliography therefore prints corrupt author names. This affects four references including the foundational Dempster-Laird-Rubin EM paper and Wu's EM-convergence paper, both load-bearing for section 05.
findings.evidence: paper.bib:117 `author = {Dempster, A.\,P. and Laird, N.\,M. and Rubin, D.\,B.}` renders in paper.bbl:78 as 'P.~Dempster, A.\, M.~Laird, N.\, and B.~Rubin, D.\,' (initials detached and reordered). Same defect at paper.bib:38 (Blom -> paper.bbl:38 'P.~Blom, Henk~A.\ and Y.~Bar-Shalom'), paper.bib:174 (Wu -> paper.bbl:98 'F.~J. Wu, C.\,'), paper.bib:206 (Anderson -> paper.bbl:109 'O.~Anderson, Brian~D.\ and J.~B. Moore').
findings.suggestion: Remove the thin-space: write initials as 'A.P.' or 'A. P.' (e.g. `author = {Dempster, A. P. and Laird, N. M. and Rubin, D. B.}`). Same fix for Blom (Henk A. P.), Wu (C. F. Jeff), Anderson (Brian D. O.). Then re-run bibtex and check the rendered names.

findings.title: pieczynski_triplet_2002: citekey year (2002) contradicts year field (2005); venue likely wrong
findings.description: The entry key encodes '2002' but the year field is {2005}, and the .bbl prints 2005. 'On Triplet Markov Chains' by Pieczynski and Desbouvries is the well-known 2002 work (ICASSP / Comptes Rendus Mathematique 2002). The entry assigns it to the ASMDA symposium with year 2005, which conflates two different items and at minimum makes the citekey misleading. This is one of the two PMC/TMC foundational references in the intro, so the venue should be correct.
findings.evidence: paper.bib:83 key `pieczynski_triplet_2002` but paper.bib:88 `year = {2005}`. Rendered paper.bbl:56-59: '...in Proceedings of the International Symposium on Applied Stochastic Models and Data Analysis (ASMDA), 2005.' Cited at sections/01_introduction.tex:32 `\cite{pieczynski_pairwise_2003,pieczynski_triplet_2002}`.
findings.suggestion: Reconcile the year and venue against the intended source. If it is the 2002 'On Triplet Markov Chains' (C. R. Acad. Sci. Paris / ICASSP 2002), set year={2002} and the correct booktitle/journal; if it is genuinely an ASMDA 2005 paper, rename the key to *_2005 to stop the year/key contradiction.

findings.title: Derivation depends on an unpublished companion paper (derrode_fast_2026)
findings.description: derrode_fast_2026 is an @unpublished 'Manuscript under review' and is the single most-cited reference (10 of the in-text \cite occurrences). Crucially it is cited to justify load-bearing steps of the derivation, not just for context: appendix/B_h5_derivation.tex:24 invokes it to assert that 'all quantities relevant to (H5) may be evaluated with the noise law', and sections/02/03 lean on it for the model and the unbiased filter being extended. Because the companion is not publicly available, a reviewer cannot verify the cited Markovianity/noise-law argument, which is a self-containedness problem (and the matter the user flagged as 'CS_FinaleBis' dependency).
findings.evidence: @unpublished{derrode_fast_2026, note={Manuscript under review}} (paper.bib:8-14). Cited 10x: 00_abstract.tex:11,23; 01_introduction.tex:44,49,59; 02_model_h5.tex:28,38,97; 03_filtering.tex:8,22,149; 08_conclusion.tex:10; appendix/B_h5_derivation.tex:24. At B_h5_derivation.tex:23-26 the proof step explicitly defers to '...the companion paper~\cite{derrode_fast_2026}'.
findings.suggestion: Either (a) include the cited noise-law / time-reversal Markovianity argument self-contained in Appendix B (the paper already has app:time_reversal it references alongside), so the derivation does not require an unavailable manuscript, or (b) add an arXiv/preprint identifier to derrode_fast_2026 so reviewers can access it. Update the note with a DOI/arXiv id once available.

findings.title: Schur complement used without citing the available Matrix Analysis reference
findings.description: Appendix B derives a conditional covariance 'by the Schur complement of...' but provides no citation, while horn_matrix_2013 (Horn & Johnson, Matrix Analysis) is defined in the .bib yet never cited (see dead-entries finding). The Schur-complement / Gaussian-conditioning fact is exactly what that reference supports. Adding the citation both fixes the dead entry and supports the algebraic step.
findings.evidence: appendix/B_h5_derivation.tex:74 '...is obtained by the Schur complement of'. horn_matrix_2013 defined at paper.bib:213 but '(no usage)' in grep. Compare appendix/E_joseph.tex:54 which does cite a matrix identity via `\cite[Sec.~5.3]{anderson_optimal_1979}`.
findings.suggestion: Add `\cite{horn_matrix_2013}` (optionally with a section locator) at the Schur-complement step in appendix/B_h5_derivation.tex:74.

findings.title: Incomplete venue metadata for conference/report entries (no pages, address, editors, DOIs)
findings.description: Several entries are thin on metadata relative to the rest of the bibliography. pieczynski_triplet_2002 (@inproceedings) has no pages, address, publisher, or DOI. Most journal entries also lack DOIs, while huang_extended_2017 is the only entry carrying a DOI — an inconsistency in completeness across the bibliography. IEEEtran does not require DOIs, so this is informational, but the missing conference location/pages for the triplet entry is a genuine gap. No empty-field or missing-required-field warnings were emitted by bibtex (paper.blg shows warning$ -- 0), so nothing is broken at compile time; this is purely metadata completeness.
findings.evidence: paper.bib:83-89 pieczynski_triplet_2002 has only author/title/booktitle/year. Only huang_extended_2017 (paper.bib:233) has a doi field. paper.blg line 53: 'warning$ -- 0' (no bibtex warnings).
findings.suggestion: For consistency, either add DOIs uniformly or omit them uniformly; at minimum fill in pages/address for pieczynski_triplet_2002 (and pieczynski_pairwise_2003 already has full journal coords, so the asymmetry is visible).

findings.title: Over-protected title braces vs. unprotected ones — inconsistent capitalization protection
findings.description: Capitalization protection with {} is applied inconsistently. huang_extended_2017 wraps the ENTIRE title in braces (paper.bib:226-227), which is heavier than needed and prevents IEEEtran from applying its sentence-case style; other entries protect only proper nouns/acronyms ({G}aussian, {EM}, {M}arkov). This is a style consistency issue, not a compile error. Note IEEEtran.bst already down-cases titles, so most titles correctly show only initial-letter protection; the fully-braced Huang title stands out as the lone exception.
findings.evidence: paper.bib:226 `title = {{Extended Reconstructed Sea Surface Temperature, Version 5 (ERSSTv5): ...}}` (whole title braced) vs. paper.bib:118 `{Maximum Likelihood from Incomplete Data via the {EM} Algorithm}` (selective protection). Rendered difference visible in paper.bbl:103 (Huang keeps full Title Case) vs. paper.bbl:77 (Dempster sentence-cased).
findings.suggestion: For style consistency, brace only the acronym in the Huang title (e.g. {ERSSTv5}) and let IEEEtran style the rest, OR document that you intentionally preserve dataset names verbatim. Low priority.

findings.title: Page/section locator used in only one citation (\cite[Sec.~5.3]{...})
findings.description: appendix/E_joseph.tex:54 is the only citation in the paper that uses an optional locator argument: `\cite[Sec.~5.3]{anderson_optimal_1979}`. Elsewhere book references (e.g. bar-shalom_estimation_2001 at sections/03_filtering.tex:328, 06_experiments.tex:149) are cited without page/section locators even where a specific result is invoked. Not an error — just a minor citation-style inconsistency. With \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran} and plain \cite, locators render as '[ref, Sec. 5.3]', which is fine.
findings.evidence: Only locator usage: appendix/E_joseph.tex:54. Other book cites bare: sections/03_filtering.tex:328 and sections/06_experiments.tex:149 (`\cite{bar-shalom_estimation_2001}`).
findings.suggestion: Optionally add section/page locators to the other textbook citations that point to a specific theorem/identity, for uniformity. Cosmetic.

dimensions.scope_summary: Audited the bibliography of the exactIMM paper: paper/paper.bib (21 entries), paper/paper.bbl (18 compiled bibitems), paper/paper.blg (bibtex log, 0 warnings), paper/paper.aux (\\citation/\\bibcite records), and all \\cite usages grepped across paper/sections/ (00..08), paper/appendix/ (B, E), and paper/paper.tex. Cross-checked defined-vs-cited keys, rendered author/title metadata, and self-containedness. NO broken citation keys and NO undefined-citation warnings were found (no ?? in compiled output; paper.log clean of citation/reference warnings). New findings beyond the previously-acted items: (1) 3 defined-but-uncited entries silently dropped from the compiled bibliography (ghahramani_variational_2000, horn_matrix_2013, rabiner_tutorial_1989) — likely missing citations rather than unused refs since all three are topically relevant; (2) mangled author names in the .bbl for 4 entries (Dempster, Wu, Blom, Anderson) caused by thin-space \\, inside name initials confusing IEEEtran name-parsing — HIGH because it corrupts the foundational EM references; (3) pieczynski_triplet_2002 citekey year (2002) contradicts year field/render (2005) with a likely-wrong ASMDA venue; (4) the derivation in appendix B and the model/filter sections depend on an unpublished 'under review' companion paper (derrode_fast_2026, cited 10x and used to justify a proof step at B_h5_derivation.tex:24) — self-containedness risk; plus a missing Schur-complement citation, metadata-completeness asymmetry (only 1 DOI), title-brace over-protection on Huang, and a lone citation locator. All file:line references included per finding.

findings.title: Author list and paper title disagree across paper.tex, CITATION.cff and README (single-author paper vs two-author citation)
findings.description: The LaTeX paper is single-author: paper.tex declares only \author{St\'ephane~Derrode} (lines 35-40), and the string 'Pieczynski' appears NOWHERE in any paper source (sections/, appendix/, *.tex) — confirmed by grep. But CITATION.cff's preferred-citation (the accompanying paper) lists TWO authors, Derrode AND Wojciech Pieczynski (CITATION.cff lines 44-52), and README.md line 7 says 'Stéphane Derrode & Wojciech Pieczynski'. The TITLES also disagree three ways: paper.tex title is 'Exact IMM Filtering and Semi-Supervised EM Estimation for Gaussian Jump Markov Systems' (lines 32-33); CITATION.cff preferred-citation title is 'On Fast Optimal Filtering in Gaussian Switching Systems' (line 43); README line 6 is 'On fast optimal filtering in Gaussian switching systems'. A reader/reviewer cannot tell who the authors are or what the paper is actually called.
findings.evidence: paper.tex:35 `\author{St\'ephane~Derrode%` (sole author); grep 'Pieczynski' over paper sources -> 'NOT mentioned anywhere in the paper sources'. CITATION.cff:43 title 'On Fast Optimal Filtering in Gaussian Switching Systems'; CITATION.cff:45-52 two given-names Stéphane / Wojciech. paper.tex:32 'Exact IMM Filtering and Semi-Supervised EM Estimation for Gaussian Jump Markov Systems'.
findings.suggestion: Decide the canonical author list and title and make paper.tex, CITATION.cff (preferred-citation) and README.md agree. If Pieczynski is a co-author, add him to paper.tex \author; if the submission is solo, drop him from CITATION.cff/README. Pick one title string and reuse it verbatim.

findings.title: Three top-level figure pairs in figures/ are never referenced by the paper (orphan/stale assets)
findings.description: The figures actually used by the paper all live in figures/generated/ (5 PDFs via \includegraphics, all confirmed present). The three older committed pairs at the top of figures/ — fig01_data.pdf/.png, fig03_em_restarts.pdf/.png, fig04_regime_trace.pdf/.png (dated Apr 20-27, ~0.6 MB total) — are not \includegraphics'd or \input anywhere (grep on exact filenames returns nothing). They appear to be an earlier figure set superseded by figures/generated/. They bloat the directory and can confuse a co-author about which figure is current. Note the whole paper/ tree is gitignored, so this is working-tree hygiene, not repo pollution.
findings.evidence: grep 'fig01_data|fig03_em_restarts|fig04_regime_trace' over sections/ appendix/ *.tex -> 'CONFIRMED: none of the 3 top-level figure pairs are \includegraphics'd'. ls figures/ shows them dated 2026-04-20/04-27 while figures/generated/*.pdf are 2026-05-06/07.
findings.suggestion: Delete the three superseded fig01/fig03/fig04 pairs (or move them to an archive/ subfolder) so only figures/generated/ assets remain. Confirm with the author first in case they are kept intentionally as source-of-truth originals.

findings.title: Generated table tab_bic.tex is produced but never \input by any section
findings.description: The figure/table generator emits figures/generated/tab_bic.tex (104 bytes, regenerated 2026-05-07 06:09), but no section or appendix \input's it — grep 'tab_bic' over sections/ appendix/ *.tex returns nothing, whereas the other 8 generated tables are all \input. Either a BIC table was dropped from the manuscript and the generator wasn't updated, or a planned table is missing from the text.

verdicts.title: Abstract/intro tout exact filtering; sole real-data result shows the regime-blind K=1 Kalman filter beats every GSS variant

verdict.reasoning:
MATHS lens, verified against the actual .tex. The finding's factual sub-claims about the BODY are accurate (07_real_data.tex:129-135 and :225-232: K=1 Kalman achieves lowest test MSE AND highest LL/obs; line 120/121: H5-exact's -30.09 advantage is scoped "Among the regime-aware GSS filters"). But the finding's central thesis collapses on inspection of the framing sections it accuses of overselling:

(1) The abstract makes NO empirical real-data superiority claim. "runs without any approximation" (00_abstract.tex:18-20) is a mathematical/algebraic exactness statement about the filter recursion (proven under H5), not a claim that the filter empirically beats baselines. Its sole empirical statement is line 39-40: the methodology is "illustrated on the NOAA SST anomalies" — "illustrated," not "shown superior." There is no claim of "the framework is the operative win on real data" to contradict; the suggested fix targets a sentence the abstract does not contain.

(2) The conclusion (08_conclusion.tex:26-29) makes only one real-data empirical claim — that the AB constraint is "numerically benign in all configurations" — which is about numerical robustness, not filter accuracy. This is fully supported (kappa(G)>1e8 robustness, lines 172-180, 232-239). No accuracy-superiority claim exists to be undercut by the K=1 result.

(3) The qualifier is NOT "buried in the body." The K=1 dominance and the "among regime-aware filters" scoping appear TWICE: in the result paragraph (129-135, explicitly called "an important caveat") and prominently in the Summary subsection (225-232). The body is scrupulously honest.

So the finding rests on conflating an algebraic exactness claim ("approximation unnecessary," true) with an empirical-performance claim, and on asserting concealment where the paper is in fact forthright. A methods paper's abstract/conclusion is not obligated to report that a textbook baseline edges out the method on one illustrative dataset, particularly when neither section makes a competing empirical claim. This is normal editorial altitude, not a coherence defect. Under "en cas de doute sérieux, isReal=false," and given the misreading at the finding's core, this is not a genuine defect; "high" severity is unjustified.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Would be at most low (cosmetic), not high: the only defensible kernel is that abstract/conclusion omit the K=1 caveat — but neither makes a contradicting empirical claim and the body states the caveat twice. Recommend isReal=false.

verdicts.title: Abstract/intro tout exact filtering; sole real-data result shows the regime-blind K=1 Kalman filter beats every GSS variant

verdict.reasoning: CONTEXTE lens. The cited facts check out: abstract says the IMM cycle "runs without any approximation" (00_abstract.tex:18-20); conclusion says the H5 class "admits a fast, exact optimal filter" (08_conclusion.tex:10); and on ENSO the K=1 Kalman filter does achieve both the lowest test MSE and the highest log-likelihood/obs, beating every regime-aware GSS variant, with the exact filter's "best test log-likelihood = -30.09" holding only among regime-aware filters (07_real_data.tex:120-135, 228-231). So the literal observation is real. BUT under the CONTEXTE lens this is a defensible editorial choice, not a misleading overclaim, and "high" is badly miscalibrated, for three reasons. (1) The caveat is NOT buried: Sec 7 states the K=1 dominance twice, prominently — once in the filter-comparison subsection ("an important caveat for interpreting the regime-aware results", lines 129-135) and again in the chapter Summary ("the single Kalman filter (K=1) achieves both a lower MSE and a higher ... log-likelihood ... than any GSS variant, reflecting the modest regime heterogeneity", lines 228-231). This is exemplary transparency, the opposite of hiding a result. (2) The abstract/conclusion claims are EXACTNESS claims, not real-data-superiority claims. "Runs without any approximation" is a correct mathematical statement about the filter under (H5); "fast, exact optimal filter" is a property attributed to the companion paper's filter. Neither claims the GSS filter beats a textbook baseline on ENSO, so neither is factually contradicted by the K=1 result. (3) The abstract explicitly frames ENSO as an illustration of methodology ("The methodology is illustrated on the NOAA SST anomalies"), and the conclusion's only empirical claim is that "the AB constraint is numerically benign in all configurations" — it makes no regime-aware-dominance claim that would require the caveat. Foregrounding the theoretical/methodological contribution in the abstract and leaving a dataset-specific caveat to the body is standard, defensible practice for a methods paper. The kernel of value — that an abstract-only reader would miss the K=1 caveat — is a real but minor editorial-altitude nit warranting at most an optional one-line qualifier ("among regime-aware filters"), exactly as the sugg proposes; it is nowhere near a high-severity coherence defect. Hence isReal=true with severity downgraded high -> low.
verdict.severityAdjustment: high -> low

verdicts.title: Abstract sells the exact filter as removing IMM error, but simulation gains are negligible (RMSE within 0.1-0.5%) on every model

verdict.reasoning:
Toutes les citations factuelles de la trouvaille sont exactes (vérifiées dans le .tex): M1 « within 0.1% » (06_experiments.tex:161), M2 « 0.4595 vs 0.4616 » (:196, soit (0.4616-0.4595)/0.4595 = 0.46%), M3 « near-identically » (:204), CPU « ~1.4x » (:166), et « only source of suboptimality » (01_introduction.tex:24). Donc la vérification factuelle au sens MATHS passe — il n'y a AUCUNE erreur de chiffre, de ligne ou d'équation.

Mais la THÈSE centrale de la trouvaille (« contribution-vs-evidence mismatch », l'abstract « impliquerait un large gain de précision ») ne tient pas:
(1) L'abstract ne fait AUCUNE affirmation quantitative de gain de précision. Il dit que l'approximation est « unnecessary » et que le cycle IMM « runs without any approximation » — c'est une affirmation d'EXACTITUDE (correction théorique), pas de grand gain RMSE. La trouvaille concède elle-même que c'est « technically true ». Un filtre exact est une contribution légitime indépendamment de l'amplitude empirique de l'erreur éliminée: l'exactitude garantit la justesse sur TOUTES les configurations, pas seulement celles testées.
(2) Le corps est totalement transparent, pas trompeur: il dit explicitement « within 0.1% », « the IMM mixture-approximation error is negligible in the one-dimensional setting » (:162), et « the RMSE gap grows with state dimension » (:177). L'altitude de l'abstract (exactitude comme résultat structurel) est honnêtement appariée à un corps qui rapporte des écarts RMSE modestes.
(3) La trouvaille fait du cherry-picking sur le RMSE et minimise le NEES. Le bénéfice empirique démontré de l'exactitude est dans la calibration de covariance: M2 NEES 0.993 (exact) vs 1.050 (IMM), soit +5.7% de mauvaise calibration directement liée à l'approximation (:197-199). L'affirmation d'exactitude de l'abstract porte précisément sur la covariance/vraisemblance correcte — que le corps DÉMONTRE. Qualifier les gains de « essentially in the noise » / « practically harmless » ignore cette preuve de calibration.
(4) « L'abstract ne mentionne jamais le CPU » est exact mais faible: les abstracts omettent couramment les détails de benchmark, et le gain CPU est rapporté dans le corps.

C'est le scénario du choix éditorial défendable (lentille CONTEXTE): présenter l'exactitude comme la contribution, avec un corps honnête sur les écarts RMSE modestes plus de vrais avantages NEES/CPU, est défendable, pas une fausse représentation. La sévérité « high » est fortement surévaluée. En cas de doute sérieux → isReal=false.
verdict.severityAdjustment: Si conservée, serait au mieux « low »: simple suggestion d'ajouter une phrase sur le CPU/calibration comme gains pratiques. Mais le cœur de la trouvaille (abstract impliquant un large gain de précision) repose sur une mauvaise caractérisation de ce que l'abstract affirme réellement (exactitude, pas amplitude RMSE), donc isReal=false.

verdicts.title: Abstract sells the exact filter as removing IMM error, but simulation gains are negligible (RMSE within 0.1-0.5%) on every model

verdict.reasoning:
Lentille CONTEXTE (faits supposes exacts: gaps RMSE 0.1-0.5%, gain CPU ~1.4x). La trouvaille repose sur une mauvaise lecture de ce que vend reellement l'abstract. J'ai lu 00_abstract.tex, 01_introduction.tex (contributions), 06_experiments.tex:100-205 et 08_conclusion.tex.

1) L'abstract NE fait AUCUNE affirmation quantitative d'exactitude. Il dit que l'approximation de collapse gaussien est 'unnecessary' et que le filtre 'runs without any approximation' (00_abstract.tex:8-20). C'est une affirmation de CORRECTION/exactitude (covariance et vraisemblance garanties correctes, forme de Joseph PSD), pas une promesse de grand gain de RMSE. La premisse de la trouvaille ('abstract sells the exact filter as removing IMM error' implique un grand gain d'exactitude) attribue a l'abstract une affirmation qu'il ne fait pas.

2) Le corps est deja scrupuleusement honnete, exactement ce que demande le 'sugg': 06_experiments.tex:160-178 dit 'nearly identical RMSE (within 0.1%)', explique POURQUOI (le cas scalaire supprime l'avantage), et annonce que l'ecart croit avec la dimension; M2 montre un avantage reel mais modeste (0.4595 vs 0.4616) plus une meilleure calibration NEES. Il n'y a donc pas de mismatch contribution-vs-evidence: la contribution annoncee est l'exactitude (garantie de correction + machinerie structurelle/estimation H5, AB-constraint, EM), et l'evidence soutient l'exactitude.

3) Le grief 'l'abstract ne mentionne pas le gain CPU ~1.4x' est un choix editorial normal d'altitude d'abstract (chiffre dependant du materiel, detail secondaire), pas une incoherence: aucune contradiction abstract/corps, seulement une difference de niveau de detail attendue.

4) Le seul element vraiment surdimensionne ('the only source of suboptimality') est dans l'INTRO (01_introduction.tex:24), pas dans l'abstract, et fait l'objet d'une trouvaille distincte deja listee (indices 10/11 du meme JSON), pas de l'ancrage de cette trouvaille (06_experiments.tex:157-205).

Il s'agit donc d'un cadrage editorial defendable (paper d'exactitude/estimation, pas de benchmark de precision) plutot que d'un defaut de coherence reel; severite 'high' clairement injustifiee. En doute serieux, verdict isReal=false conformement au protocole.
verdict.severityAdjustment: N/A (isReal=false). Au mieux ce serait une remarque cosmetique de severite 'low' (ajouter une demi-phrase sur le gain CPU dans l'abstract), pas 'high'.

verdicts.title: Abstract lists Σ_U among the five blocks that determine A and B; body and proof state A,B are independent of Σ_U

verdict.reasoning: All three factual claims verified against the .tex files. (1) Abstract 00_abstract.tex:31-34 reads "that determines A and B jointly from the remaining five blocks (C, D, \Sigma_U, \Sigma_V, \Delta)" — Sigma_U IS listed. (2) The boxed formula eq:AB (04_constraint.tex:70-74) is A=Delta Sigma_V^{-1} C, B=Delta Sigma_V^{-1} D — contains no Sigma_U. (3) Sec 4 body (04_constraint.tex:77-79) uses the identical five-block list but appends the crucial qualifier "independently of Sigma_U", which the abstract omits. (4) Intro contribution 2 (01_introduction.tex:80-82) lists only four blocks "(C, D, \Delta, \Sigma_V)". (5) The appendix proof (C_projections.tex:73-88) confirms A,B arise via Sigma_V^{-1} only, with no Sigma_U dependence. So the three different block lists genuinely circulate as the finding states, and saying Sigma_U "determines" A,B in the abstract is mathematically imprecise: A,B are functions of (C,D,Delta,Sigma_V) only. The finding is REAL. Calibration note: the finding itself anticipates the one defensible reading — Sigma_U genuinely IS one of the five free blocks of the H5 model (the BIC count at 06_experiments.tex:373-379 treats the full joint covariance Sigma_W including Sigma_U as free). So the abstract's five-block list is not wrong as a list of free model blocks; the defect is purely the verb "determines", a framing-prose wording slip that the Sec-4 body already corrects with "independently of Sigma_U". This is a genuine editorial-coherence imprecision plus a real intro-vs-abstract inconsistency, but the underlying math and proof are fully correct and the body already carries the right qualifier — so it is a low-stakes wording fix, not a medium-severity substantive issue. Downgrading medium -> low.
verdict.severityAdjustment: medium -> low

verdicts.title: Abstract lists Σ_U among the five blocks that determine A and B; body and proof state A,B are independent of Σ_U

verdict.reasoning:
CONTEXTE lens. The underlying math facts check out: A,B genuinely do not depend on Σ_U (eq:AB at 04_constraint.tex:71-73 is A=ΔΣ_V^{-1}C, B=ΔΣ_V^{-1}D, no Σ_U), and the cross-section wording does differ — abstract (00_abstract.tex:32-33) lists 5 blocks (C,D,Σ_U,Σ_V,Δ); intro contribution 2 (01_introduction.tex:80-81) lists only 4 (C,D,Δ,Σ_V); Sec 4 body (04_constraint.tex:78-79) lists the same 5 blocks but adds 'independently of Σ_U'. So a real, low-grade harmonization issue exists.

But the finding's central charge — that the abstract is 'misleading' for listing Σ_U as a determinant — is overstated under the CONTEXTE lens. The abstract says A,B are determined 'from the remaining five blocks (C,D,Σ_U,Σ_V,Δ)'. The word 'remaining' signals these are the free model blocks left after removing A,B from the 7-block parameterization (A,B,C,D,Σ_U,Δ,Σ_V). That is a defensible and consistent description: Sec 4 (04_constraint.tex:90-92) explicitly states the H5 family 'is parametrised by the five free blocks (C,D,Σ_U,Σ_V,Δ) per regime', and the BIC count (06_experiments.tex:373-379) counts exactly these blocks (C,D, the joint covariance ΣW containing Σ_U/Δ/Σ_V, bias). Crucially, the body's PRIMARY phrasing (04_constraint.tex:78) is itself the identical 5-block list — 'uniquely determined by the remaining five blocks (C,D,Σ_U,Σ_V,Δ)' — just with a clarifying parenthetical 'independently of Σ_U' appended. So the abstract is consistent with the body's own primary wording; it merely omits the parenthetical refinement, which is a normal abstract-vs-body altitude choice. The boxed formula directly downstream shows Σ_U is absent, so no reader is durably misled.

The genuinely real inconsistency is intro (4 blocks) vs abstract/body (5 blocks), a one-line wording harmonization. That warrants a low-severity flag, not medium. The suggested fix (use one consistent statement; note Σ_U is a free block but does not enter A,B) is reasonable and the issue is real, hence isReal=true, but 'misleading' / medium overstates a cosmetic cross-reference nit. Downgrade to low.
verdict.severityAdjustment: medium -> low

verdicts.title: BIC model-order-selection subsection presents a method but delivers no result; only one BIC number, K=1,3,4 deferred

verdict.reasoning: Every factual claim in the finding checks out against the real .tex (06_experiments.tex). (1) The BIC material IS a top-level numbered subsection: line 362 \subsection{Model order selection via BIC} (verified via grep of all subsections; it is the last subsection of Sec 6). (2) It DOES present a full apparatus: parameter-count formula eq:bic_d (lines 367-372), the worked count d=17 (line 380), and the BIC definition (line 382). (3) It DOES report only one number, BIC_2 = 1846.8 (line 387), for the true K=2 model. (4) It DOES explicitly defer K=1,3,4: lines 389-393 quote verbatim 'A proper BIC-based model order selection requires fitting H5-GSS models with K=1,3,4 ... this is deferred to a companion computational study and does not affect the theoretical contributions of the present paper.' With no K!=2 values, no selection is performed, exactly as the finding states. (5) Verified the intro's four contributions (01_introduction.tex:57-100: IMM rewriting, AB constraint, OLS, EM) and the entire conclusion incl. future-work items (i)-(iv) (08_conclusion.tex) — neither mentions BIC or model selection. A grep for BIC/model order over all sections+appendix confirms every mention is confined to this one subsection. So the finding is real and not a misreading. Applying the adversarial MATHS lens I find no error in the finding itself; the eq:bic_d formula it describes is in fact correct (yields the verified d=17). The one cosmetic imprecision is the '6.6' label, which is immaterial (structurally it is Sec 6's final subsection). On calibration, two mitigating factors argue for lowering severity from medium: (a) the subsection carries an explicit, honest in-text disclaimer that the deferral 'does not affect the theoretical contributions of the present paper', so the paper does not actually overclaim a selection result; and (b) this is purely an editorial-structuring/altitude matter with zero mathematical or factual defect — the parameter-count machinery is correct and reusable. The finding's own remedy (demote to a remark) is reasonable. Hence isReal=true with severity medium -> low.
verdict.severityAdjustment: medium -> low

verdicts.title: BIC model-order-selection subsection presents a method but delivers no result; only one BIC number, K=1,3,4 deferred

verdict.reasoning:
CONTEXTE lens, finding REFUTED as a genuine coherence defect (treated as non-issue / over-severe).

Facts verified against 06_experiments.tex:362-393, 01_introduction.tex:54-100, 08_conclusion.tex: the evidence quotes are all accurate. Section 6.6 "Model order selection via BIC" exists, gives eq:bic_d, the BIC definition, a single value BIC2=1846.8 for the true K=2 model, and explicitly defers the K=1,3,4 sweep. The four intro contributions (filter rewriting, AB-constraint study, supervised OLS, semi-supervised EM) indeed do not list model selection, and the conclusion does not mention BIC.

Why this is a defensible editorial choice and not a real coherence defect:
1) The subsection opens "We recall that the number of free parameters..." — the count is presented as recalled/derived, not as a "full BIC apparatus" claimed as a contribution. The text never asserts model selection as a contribution, so there is NO abstract/intro-vs-body altitude mismatch (the auditor itself confirms BIC is unadvertised). The framing-overclaim mechanism that would make this a coherence defect is absent.
2) Crucially, the same subsection contains an explicit in-text disclaimer (lines 392-393): the full selection "is deferred to a companion computational study and does not affect the theoretical contributions of the present paper." This honest, co-located withdrawal directly neutralizes the finding's central charge that the subsection "promises selection and delivers none." The promise is transparently retracted in the same breath; a reader is not misled.
3) Reporting a parameter-count formula plus a representative BIC value for the true model, while flagging the comparison sweep as future work, is squarely within acceptable scientific writing. The auditor's own suggestion is a pure either/or editorial preference ("complete the sweep OR demote to a remark"), which signals a style nitpick rather than an incoherence.

The only residual kernel: the subsection TITLE ("Model order selection via BIC") over-promises relative to content, which a TOC-scanner might find mildly misleading before reaching the disclaimer. That is a low-severity cosmetic point, not the "medium" editorial-coherence defect claimed. Given the explicit self-disclaimer and the absence of any framing mismatch, under the CONTEXTE lens "en cas de doute sérieux, isReal=false" applies: this is a defensible editorial choice, so isReal=false.
verdict.severityAdjustment: If retained at all, downgrade from medium to low (cosmetic title-vs-content nitpick), since the in-text deferral disclaimer removes the substantive coherence concern.

verdicts.title: 'generically necessary' claim survives in abstract+intro+conclusion with no supporting argument anywhere (counting argument absent from Appendix C)

verdict.reasoning:
MATHS-lens verification against the actual .tex confirms every factual claim of the finding.

Verbatim checks: abstract (00_abstract.tex:33-34) reads "...and is sufficient — and generically necessary — for (H5)-compatibility" with NO qualifier; conclusion (08_conclusion.tex:17-19) reads "This form is always sufficient for (H5) and generically necessary in all configurations considered in the experiments" — also dropping the inequality. The introduction (01_introduction.tex:83-84) is the ONLY place that qualifies it: "always sufficient ... and generically necessary when K·s >= q+s, i.e., in all configurations considered in the experiments."

Appendix C (C_projections.tex, label app:AB_constraint — the appendix cited as justification) contains exactly two things: (1) a "necessity" elimination argument whose premise is that eq:H5_in_X must hold "across the cone of admissible Σ(r) ≻ 0" / "for all Σ(r) ≻ 0" (lines 46-61), forcing X(r)=H^T Σ_V^{-1} Δ^T; and (2) the sufficiency Proposition (lines 69-92, direct substitution). verdict.reasoning: Verified factually against the actual file. paper/paper.tex line 22 sits exactly between \usepackage{hyperref} (line 21) and \input{macros} (line 23), as claimed. od -c on line 22 shows it consists of a single space character followed by newline ( \n), and awk confirms length==1. grep -nP ' $' over the whole file returns only '22: ', confirming it is the sole trailing-whitespace line in the master file — matching the cited evidence (grep -n ' $' -> '22: ') exactly. Every factual assertion in the finding is reproducible and correct. Severity 'info' is appropriately calibrated: this is a trivial, cosmetic, non-compiling working-tree hygiene item (a blank-with-space line has no typesetting effect in a LaTeX preamble). The suggested fix (strip the trailing space) is correct and harmless. Nothing to refute under the MATHS lens.
